cases.rules_text:
1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.
14. The restricted shelf access article covers restricted shelf violation; the prescribed consequence is an archive warning.
15. The relic handling breach article covers relic handling violation; the prescribed consequence is reading room suspension.
16. The catalog alteration article covers catalog alteration violation; the prescribed consequence is conservation quarantine.
17. The vault removal article covers vault removal; the prescribed consequence is permanent archive exclusion.
18. The curator order authorization article covers curator order authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
19. The flood rescue authorization article covers flood rescue authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. Where the relic handling breach article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the relic handling breach article prevails.
21. Where the catalog alteration article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the catalog alteration article prevails.
22. Where the vault removal article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
23. Where the vault removal article and the relic handling breach article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
24. Where the vault removal article and the catalog alteration article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
25. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
26. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
27. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the catalog alteration article when both apply.
28. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
29. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
30. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the vault removal article when both apply.
cases.case_text: Alex entered a closed stack. The shelf was marked restricted. No curator was present. The relic was not tagged fragile. The catalog lock was not active. The exit alarm was not active. Alex had no written curator order. No flood alarm sounded.

cases.rules_text:
1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.
14. The restricted shelf access article covers restricted shelf violation; the prescribed consequence is an archive warning.
15. The relic handling breach article covers relic handling violation; the prescribed consequence is reading room suspension.
16. The catalog alteration article covers catalog alteration violation; the prescribed consequence is conservation quarantine.
17. The vault removal article covers vault removal; the prescribed consequence is permanent archive exclusion.
18. The curator order authorization article covers curator order authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
19. The flood rescue authorization article covers flood rescue authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. Where the relic handling breach article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the relic handling breach article prevails.
21. Where the catalog alteration article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the catalog alteration article prevails.
22. Where the vault removal article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
23. Where the vault removal article and the relic handling breach article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
24. Where the vault removal article and the catalog alteration article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
25. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
26. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
27. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the catalog alteration article when both apply.
28. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
29. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
30. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the vault removal article when both apply.

1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.
14. The restricted shelf access article covers restricted shelf violation; the prescribed consequence is an archive warning.
15. The relic handling breach article covers relic handling violation; the prescribed consequence is reading room suspension.
16. The catalog alteration article covers catalog alteration violation; the prescribed consequence is conservation quarantine.
17. The vault removal article covers vault removal; the prescribed consequence is permanent archive exclusion.
18. The curator order authorization article covers curator order authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
19. The flood rescue authorization article covers flood rescue authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. Where the relic handling breach article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the relic handling breach article prevails.
21. Where the catalog alteration article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the catalog alteration article prevails.
22. Where the vault removal article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
23. Where the vault removal article and the relic handling breach article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
24. Where the vault removal article and the catalog alteration article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.

26. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
27. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the catalog alteration article when both apply.
28. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
29. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
30. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the vault removal article when both apply.

cases.rules_text:
1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.
14. The restricted shelf access article covers restricted shelf violation; the prescribed consequence is an archive warning.
15. The relic handling breach article covers relic handling violation; the prescribed consequence is reading room suspension.
16. The catalog alteration article covers catalog alteration violation; the prescribed consequence is conservation quarantine.
17. The vault removal article covers vault removal; the prescribed consequence is permanent archive exclusion.
18. The curator order authorization article covers curator order authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
19. The flood rescue authorization article covers flood rescue authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. Where the relic handling breach article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the relic handling breach article prevails.
21. Where the catalog alteration article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the catalog alteration article prevails.
22. Where the vault removal article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
23. Where the vault removal article and the relic handling breach article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.

27. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the catalog alteration article when both apply.
28. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
29. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
30. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the vault removal article when both apply.

1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.

cases.rules_text:
1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.
14. The restricted shelf access article covers restricted shelf violation; the prescribed consequence is an archive warning.
15. The relic handling breach article covers relic handling violation; the prescribed consequence is reading room suspension.
16. The catalog alteration article covers catalog alteration violation; the prescribed consequence is conservation quarantine.
17. The vault removal article covers vault removal; the prescribed consequence is permanent archive exclusion.
18. The curator order authorization article covers curator order authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
19. The flood rescue authorization article covers flood rescue authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. Where the relic handling breach article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the relic handling breach article prevails.
21. Where the catalog alteration article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the catalog alteration article prevails.
22. Where the vault removal article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
23. Where the vault removal article and the relic handling breach article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
24. Where the vault removal article and the catalog alteration article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
25. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
26. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
27. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the catalog alteration article when both apply.
28. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
29. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
30. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the vault removal article when both apply.

cases.rules_text:
1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.
14. The restricted shelf access article covers restricted shelf violation; the prescribed consequence is an archive warning.
15. The relic handling breach article covers relic handling violation; the prescribed consequence is reading room suspension.
16. The catalog alteration article covers catalog alteration violation; the prescribed consequence is conservation quarantine.
17. The vault removal article covers vault removal; the prescribed consequence is permanent archive exclusion.
18. The curator order authorization article covers curator order authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
19. The flood rescue authorization article covers flood rescue authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. Where the relic handling breach article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the relic handling breach article prevails.
21. Where the catalog alteration article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the catalog alteration article prevails.
22. Where the vault removal article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
23. Where the vault removal article and the relic handling breach article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
24. Where the vault removal article and the catalog alteration article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
25. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
26. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
27. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the catalog alteration article when both apply.
28. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
29. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
30. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the vault removal article when both apply.
cases.case_text: Jamie bypassed the reading-room desk. The shelf was marked restricted. Jamie placed a relic under a lamp. The relic was tagged fragile. Jamie swapped an archive label. The catalog lock was active. Jamie left the building with an archive box. The exit alarm was active. A flood alarm sounded. Jamie moved the item to a dry room. Jamie filed a rescue note. No curator was present. Jamie had no written curator order.

cases.rules_text:
1. Unauthorized stack entry is present if any one of the following occurs: a person enters a closed stack, uses an unregistered reader card, or bypasses the reading-room desk.
2. Restricted shelf status is present when the shelf is marked restricted and no curator is present.
3. Fragile relic handling is present when the relic is tagged fragile and any one of the following occurs: the person touches it without gloves, lifts it from its cradle, or places it under a lamp.
4. Catalog record interference is present when the catalog lock is active and any one of the following occurs: the person changes an entry, deletes a condition note, or swaps an archive label.
5. A vault exit step is present when the exit alarm is active and any one of the following occurs: the person carries the relic past the blue line, seals it in a private case, or leaves with an archive box.
6. Curator authorization records are complete when there is a written curator order, the order names the relic, and the handling time is logged.
7. Flood rescue records are complete when a flood alarm sounds, the item is moved to a dry room, and a rescue note is filed.
8. A restricted shelf violation is established by unauthorized stack entry into a restricted shelf area.
9. A relic handling violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with fragile relic handling.
10. A catalog alteration violation is established when a restricted shelf violation is combined with catalog record interference.
11. Vault removal is established when a relic handling violation is followed by a vault exit step.
12. Curator order authorization is established when relic handling is supported by complete curator authorization records.
13. Flood rescue authorization is established when restricted shelf conduct is supported by complete flood rescue records.
14. The restricted shelf access article covers restricted shelf violation; the prescribed consequence is an archive warning.
15. The relic handling breach article covers relic handling violation; the prescribed consequence is reading room suspension.
16. The catalog alteration article covers catalog alteration violation; the prescribed consequence is conservation quarantine.
17. The vault removal article covers vault removal; the prescribed consequence is permanent archive exclusion.
18. The curator order authorization article covers curator order authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
19. The flood rescue authorization article covers flood rescue authorization; the prescribed consequence is no criminal liability.
20. Where the relic handling breach article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the relic handling breach article prevails.
21. Where the catalog alteration article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the catalog alteration article prevails.
22. Where the vault removal article and the restricted shelf access article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
23. Where the vault removal article and the relic handling breach article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
24. Where the vault removal article and the catalog alteration article both apply, the vault removal article prevails.
25. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
26. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
27. The curator order authorization article operates as an exception to the catalog alteration article when both apply.
28. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the restricted shelf access article when both apply.
29. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the relic handling breach article when both apply.
30. The flood rescue authorization article operates as an exception to the vault removal article when both apply.
